steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Command of Evidence question. The student's claim is that countries with very large populations can be less densely populated than countries with much smaller populations. To support this, the answer must provide a direct comparison showing one country with a high population but low density paired against one with a low population but high density — both data points are required.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — the geographic size of Angola (1,246,700 square kilometers) with its relatively high population of 25,022,000 inhabitants.: This only describes Angola's geographic size and population without mentioning density, and it never introduces a second country to compare. There is no contrast that supports the claim. ✗
  • Choice B — Angola, which has a high population of 25,022,000 inhabitants and a relatively low density of 20.1 inhabitants/square kilometer, with Sao Tomé and Principe, which has a much lower population and a higher density.: Angola has a large population (25,022,000) but a low density (20.1 per km²). Sao Tomé and Principe has a much smaller population but a higher density. This direct comparison is exactly the evidence needed to support the claim. ✓
  • Choice C — Mauritania, which has a low density, with Ethiopia, which has a similar density despite both countries having different geographic sizes.: Comparing two countries that have similar densities does the opposite of supporting the claim — it shows no contrast in density despite size differences. ✗
  • Choice D — the populations of both Sao Tomé and Principe and Ethiopia in 2015 with their populations in 2010.: Comparing populations across time for the same countries is irrelevant to the density-versus-population-size claim. ✗
B is correct.
